schoolName: University of Malta - Faculty of Medicine and Surgery

sources.label: University of Malta official site - Doctor of Medicine and Surgery (UMDFT) course overview
sources.notes: 5-year, 300 ECTS MD programme; special entry requirements (A-level Biology and Chemistry Grade B+); Medical Maltese proficiency requirement for non-Maltese applicants; annual tuition fee EUR 26,000 for international students; occupational health assessment requirement; limited registration with the Malta Medical Council on graduation.

sources.label: University of Malta official site - Faculty of Medicine & Surgery home
sources.notes: Faculty landing page confirming Department of Medicine, history link, and general faculty structure.

sources.label: University of Malta official site - Faculty history
sources.notes: Faculty formally instituted as the Collegio Medico on 25 May 1771, one of the original faculties of the Pubblica Universita di Studi Generali (est. 22 November 1769); teaching interrupted under Napoleonic decree in 1798 and resumed in 1800.

sources.label: University of Malta official site - Country-specific qualifications: India
sources.notes: Indian Class-XII / HSC / All India Senior School Certificate holders are directed to the University's dedicated International School for Foundation Studies pathway programmes rather than being confirmed for direct entry into the MD course.

sources.label: University of Malta official site - Certificate in Foundation Studies in Medical and Dental Sciences overview
sources.notes: One-year full-time foundation course (human biology, biological chemistry, human anatomy, genetics, cell biology, Medical English and Maltese) for international applicants whose school system is not comparable to the Maltese system, designed to allow progression onto the MD and Master of Dentistry courses; entry via interview and/or written test at the Board's discretion.

sources.label: University of Malta official site - International student accommodation
sources.notes: University recommends Campus Hub Residence (next to the Msida campus, operated by a third party, not a University/health-corporation-run facility) for international students, largely because it simplifies residence-permit paperwork versus private rentals.

sources.label: Wikipedia - University of Malta
sources.notes: Independent corroboration: university traces roots to 1592, campus location Tal-Qroqq, Msida; English as language of instruction; ~11,500 students (2023); EUA/ACU/Utrecht Network affiliations.

sources.label: Beyond The States - Msida, Malta location profile
sources.notes: Independent corroboration: Msida is a small coastal town ~2.5km from Valletta, central/harbour-area location, quieter residential character relative to nearby Sliema/St Julian's nightlife districts, generally lower living costs than Western/Northern European capitals.

sources.label: GMC (UK) visit report - Barts and The London School of Medicine and Dentistry / QMUL Malta MBBS programme
sources.notes: Corroborates that the Queen Mary University of London (Barts) Malta MBBS programme on Gozo is a distinct UK-degree-awarding programme, separate from the University of Malta's own MD course, avoiding conflation between the two Malta medical pathways.

sources.label: Numbeo - Crime Index by Country (2026 mid-year)
sources.notes: Independent data point: Malta's crime index (~39-43, 'low-to-moderate') sits below the European average (~52), used as general safety context rather than a University of Malta claim.

structuredFacts.name: University of Malta - Faculty of Medicine and Surgery
bestFitFor: Indian students who can complete (or already hold) a recognised foundation/bridging pathway into an English-medium EU medical degree, since direct entry from an Indian Class-XII certificate is not confirmed and a University of Malta foundation-year route is the documented pathway
Students who are prepared to also complete the university's Medical Maltese proficiency requirement (or an exemption route) alongside their medical studies, not only English-medium coursework
Students who want a small, English-speaking EU island nation with an old, established medical faculty (18th-century origins) and modern EU/ASIIN-linked quality accreditation

Students comfortable budgeting for a relatively high annual tuition fee (EUR 26,000 for international students) and Malta's cost of living, offset against potentially shorter travel and strong Mediterranean/EU clinical exposure
Students who plan to independently verify NMC/NEET/FMGE recognition requirements before enrolling, rather than assuming automatic Indian recognition

programs.title: Doctor of Medicine and Surgery (M.D.)
programs.medium: English (medical-content instruction), with a Medical Maltese proficiency requirement for all students -- a Secondary Education Certificate pass in Maltese, or the University's Medical Maltese Proficiency Certificate (or the Medical Maltese study-unit within the Foundation course), is required before starting Year 1 and again before Year 3, unless a recognised exemption applies
programs.notes: 300-ECTS, 5-year MD split into pre-clinical and clinical phases, with an optional intercalated BSc (Hons) in Medical Sciences after Year 2. Special course entry requirements (as listed for international qualifications) include two A-level passes at Grade B or better in Biology and Chemistry plus an Intermediate-level pass at Grade B or better in another subject, from one sitting. For Indian Class-XII / HSC / All India Senior School Certificate holders, the University's official India-specific admissions guidance points to its International School for Foundation Studies pathway programmes rather than confirming direct entry -- the relevant one-year full-time route is the Certificate in Foundation Studies in Medical and Dental Sciences, admission to which is at the Board's discretion (interview and/or written test). Graduates become eligible for limited registration with the Malta Medical Council and can apply for foundation doctor posts; the course holds AMSE and ASIIN quality-seal accreditation per the official course page. Annual tuition fee for international (non-EU) students is listed as EUR 26,000 on the official course page; students should reconfirm the current-year figure directly with the University, and separately verify Indian NMC/NEET-eligibility and FMGE/NExT requirements before enrolling, since recognition of any foreign medical degree in India is assessed against NMC's own regulations rather than granted automatically.

structuredFacts.researchNotes: Public, state-run University of Malta; its Faculty of Medicine and Surgery traces institutional roots to the Collegio Medico founded 25 May 1771 as one of the original faculties of the Pubblica Universita di Studi Generali (est. 1769); the university overall traces its lineage to 1592 per Wikipedia/secondary sourcing. Main campus is at Tal-Qroqq, Msida, a small coastal town on Malta's northeast harbour area, about 2.5km from the capital Valletta and close to Sliema/St Julian's. The MD (Doctor of Medicine and Surgery) is a 5-year, 300-ECTS programme; content is taught in English but ALL students (including international ones) must satisfy a Medical Maltese language requirement before Year 1 and Year 3 unless exempted -- this is a genuine, non-trivial caveat for Indian applicants and must not be downplayed. Critically, the University's own India-specific admissions page does NOT confirm direct entry into the MD course for Indian Class-XII/HSC holders; it instead routes them to foundation-year pathway programmes (specifically the one-year Certificate in Foundation Studies in Medical and Dental Sciences for medicine/dentistry entry), with admission to that foundation course itself discretionary via interview/written test. This is an important, non-obvious structural fact that materially changes the applicant journey versus many MBBS-abroad destinations with direct XII-to-MBBS entry, and the page must state it plainly rather than imply direct MBBS admission from Class XII. Do not conflate this university with the separate Queen Mary University of London (Barts and The London) Malta MBBS/MBBCh programme based on Gozo, which awards a different (UK/QMUL) degree under a different admissions process -- confirmed distinct via GMC visit-report sourcing. Tuition is EUR 26,000/year for international students per the official course page at time of research; this is materially higher than several other popular MBBS-abroad destinations and should be flagged as a genuine cost consideration, not omitted. NMC/NEET/FMGE: standard India-outbound framework applies (WDOMS listing generally required for NMC recognition pathway; FMGE/NExT screening required to practise in India) -- independent secondary sourcing corroborates the University features in ECFMG/WDOMS-linked recognition discussions, but this page must instruct students to verify current-year NMC status directly rather than asserting blanket approval, since NMC's approved-institution list changes and was not directly retrievable from NMC's own site during this research pass. Housing: the University does not run its own halls of residence; it recommends the privately operated Campus Hub Residence adjacent to the Msida campus (chosen mainly because it simplifies residence-permit paperwork versus private rentals), with indicative shared-room pricing around EUR 600/month per independent secondary sourcing -- this must be presented as a recommended third-party option, not a University-run hostel. Indian food access: Malta has a modest but real Indian restaurant presence concentrated in Sliema (e.g., named restaurants found via independent sourcing) a short distance from Msida, though no evidence of a large established Indian-student community comparable to bigger European hubs was found, so this is framed honestly as limited-but-present rather than absent or extensive. Safety: Malta is broadly framed as a safe, low-to-moderate crime EU country per independent Numbeo-style indices, used as general context rather than a University claim.

draftContent.summary: The University of Malta's Faculty of Medicine and Surgery, based on the university's main Tal-Qroqq campus in Msida, is one of the oldest continuously evolving medical faculties in the Mediterranean, tracing its roots to the Collegio Medico founded in 1771. It is a public, state faculty offering a 5-year, 300-ECTS Doctor of Medicine and Surgery (MD) degree, taught primarily in English but carrying a Medical Maltese language requirement for every student, including international ones, unless a specific exemption applies. For Indian students, the university's own India-specific admissions guidance does not confirm direct entry from a Class-XII / HSC certificate into the MD course; instead it points applicants toward its International School for Foundation Studies, and specifically a one-year Certificate in Foundation Studies in Medical and Dental Sciences, as the documented route into medicine, with admission to that foundation year itself discretionary. Annual tuition for international students is listed at EUR 26,000, a materially higher figure than several other popular MBBS-abroad destinations. Students considering this route should independently verify current Medical Maltese requirements, foundation-pathway admission criteria, and Indian NMC/NEET/FMGE recognition status directly with the University and with Indian regulators before applying, rather than assuming a straightforward Class-XII-to-MBBS pipeline.
draftContent.campusLifestyle: The Faculty of Medicine and Surgery sits within the University of Malta's main Tal-Qroqq campus in Msida, a single urban campus serving the university's roughly 11,500 students across its faculties. Medical students share general campus infrastructure -- lecture halls, libraries, and recreational facilities -- with the wider student body, and the university is affiliated with international bodies including the European University Association and the Association of Commonwealth Universities, reflecting an internationally networked, EU-integrated academic environment. English is the primary language of campus and coursework instruction, which eases day-to-day academic life for international students, even though the Medical Maltese requirement remains a distinct hurdle specific to the medical course. Msida's central, harbour-area location keeps the campus a short distance from the busier Sliema and St Julian's districts, giving students access to nearby dining, waterfront areas, and nightlife without living directly inside a large capital city.
draftContent.cityProfile: Msida is a small coastal town on Malta's northeast harbour area, roughly 2.5 kilometres from the capital, Valletta, and it is best known regionally as the seat of the University of Malta's main campus. Its character is generally described as quieter and more residential than neighbouring Sliema and St Julian's, which sit close by and supply most of the area's cafes, restaurants, and nightlife. Malta's Mediterranean climate brings hot, dry summers and short, cool winters, and the country's compact size means Msida-based students can reach Valletta, Sliema, and other coastal towns quickly by bus or short taxi ride. Cost of living in Msida is generally presented as more affordable than major Western or Northern European university cities, though students should still budget for Malta's EU cost-of-living levels rather than assuming South Asian-level costs. As English is one of Malta's two official languages alongside Maltese, everyday communication -- shopping, banking, healthcare -- is broadly accessible without needing Maltese fluency, even though the MD course itself carries its own Medical Maltese academic requirement.
draftContent.clinicalExposure: The MD programme is structured into a pre-clinical phase and a clinical phase across its 5 years and 300 ECTS credits, combining traditional lectures, tutorials, and small-group teaching with problem-based learning where appropriate. Pre-clinical years include hands-on practical sessions such as anatomy dissection, while the clinical years introduce bedside teaching, shadowing and observation, structured skills training, and theatre and ward attendance, per the official course description. Students who wish to deepen laboratory-based research experience can opt into an intercalated year after the pre-clinical phase, leading to a separate Bachelor of Science (Honours) in Medical Sciences degree -- a route the university positions as useful for students considering a future physician-scientist career track. Specific teaching-hospital placements and rotation sites were not independently itemised in this research pass; applicants should confirm current clinical placement arrangements directly with the Faculty of Medicine and Surgery before enrolling.
draftContent.hostelOverview: The University of Malta does not operate its own on-campus halls of residence for medical students; instead, it directs international students toward Campus Hub Residence, a privately operated student residence located next to the Msida campus. The university's own accommodation guidance recommends Campus Hub specifically because booking through it simplifies the paperwork (such as Housing Authority documentation) needed for an e-Residence Permit application, compared with arranging a private rental independently. Campus Hub is explicitly described by the university as operated by a third party, with the University and Malta's health-education bodies bearing no responsibility for events or conditions on the property -- so students should treat it as a recommended external option rather than a university-run hostel, and should verify current room types, pricing, and availability directly with Campus Hub or the University's accommodation office before booking.
draftContent.indianFoodSupport: Malta does not have a large, long-established Indian student or diaspora community on the scale of bigger European university hubs, so students should not expect an extensive Indian grocery and restaurant ecosystem comparable to those cities. That said, a genuine, modest Indian restaurant presence exists within easy reach of Msida, concentrated in the nearby Sliema area, giving students realistic options for eating out rather than none at all. Self-catering students can supplement home cooking with international-aisle staples available in Maltese supermarkets, and Campus Hub Residence includes a supermarket on site. Students who rely heavily on specific Indian spices or ingredients should expect to source some items from specialty stores or occasional trips, and should connect with any existing Indian or South Asian student networks at the University of Malta for current, first-hand guidance before arrival.
draftContent.safetyOverview: Malta is broadly regarded as a safe EU destination for international students, with independent crime-index data placing the country below the European average on measures of reported crime. Msida's residential, harbour-area setting is generally described as calmer than the adjacent nightlife districts of Sliema and St Julian's, and English-language signage and services across Malta make it straightforward for international students to navigate daily life, healthcare, and public services. As with any destination, students should still follow standard personal-safety practices, register their residence status as required under Maltese immigration rules, and stay in touch with the Indian High Commission's coverage for Malta (via its nearest accredited mission) for updated consular guidance specific to their stay.
draftContent.studentSupport: International applicants are directed to the University of Malta's dedicated country-specific admissions guidance -- including an India-specific page -- and to its International School for Foundation Studies for students whose Class-XII/HSC qualification requires a bridging year before medicine. The university's accommodation office administers the recommended Campus Hub Residence booking and residence-permit paperwork process for incoming international students. Faculty-specific queries for the MD course, including questions on the Medical Maltese requirement, special entry requirements, and the intercalated BSc (Hons) option, should be directed to the Faculty of Medicine and Surgery directly. Indian applicants should additionally plan to consult India's nearest accredited diplomatic mission covering Malta for visa and consular support, and must independently verify current NMC recognition, NEET eligibility, and FMGE/NExT requirements with Indian regulators before enrolling, since this page does not assert automatic Indian recognition of the MD degree.
whyChoose: One of the oldest medical faculties in the Mediterranean, with institutional roots dating to the Collegio Medico founded in 1771, combined with modern AMSE and ASIIN quality-seal accreditation on its MD course
English-medium coursework and campus life on an EU island nation, with an optional intercalated BSc (Hons) in Medical Sciences for students interested in a research-oriented physician-scientist track
Structured 5-year, 300-ECTS MD curriculum blending pre-clinical dissection-based anatomy training with clinical-phase bedside teaching, shadowing, skills training, and theatre/ward attendance
Compact, walkable Msida location roughly 2.5km from the capital Valletta, with quick access to the livelier Sliema and St Julian's areas and Malta's broader EU/Mediterranean setting

A public, state university structure rather than a purely private for-profit medical school, situated within a small, English-speaking, and by regional crime-index measures comparatively safe EU country
thingsToConsider: Direct entry from an Indian Class-XII / HSC / All India Senior School Certificate into the MD course is not confirmed by the University's own India-specific guidance; the documented route is via a foundation-year pathway (the Certificate in Foundation Studies in Medical and Dental Sciences), and admission to that foundation year is itself discretionary (interview and/or written test)

All students -- including international ones -- must satisfy a Medical Maltese language requirement (a Maltese SEC pass, the Medical Maltese Proficiency Certificate, or an approved foundation-course unit) before Year 1 and again before Year 3, unless specifically exempted; this is a genuine extra academic hurdle beyond English-medium coursework

Annual tuition for international students is listed at EUR 26,000 on the official course page -- a materially higher figure than several other popular MBBS-abroad destinations -- and students should reconfirm the current-year fee directly with the University before budgeting

The University does not run its own medical-student halls of residence; the officially recommended Campus Hub Residence is a privately operated facility, and the University explicitly disclaims responsibility for conditions there, so students should treat it as a recommended third-party option and verify terms independently

Recognition of the MD degree by India's National Medical Commission, NEET eligibility, and the FMGE/NExT licensing exam requirement are governed by NMC's own regulations, which can change; this page does not assert automatic Indian recognition, and students must verify current status with NMC directly before enrolling

Do not confuse this University of Malta MD programme with the separate Queen Mary University of London (Barts and The London) MBBS programme based on Gozo, which is a distinct UK-degree pathway with its own admissions process and campus

Malta has only a modest Indian student community and Indian food scene compared with larger European study hubs, so day-to-day cultural and culinary familiarity will be more limited than in bigger destinations

faq.question: Can an Indian student join the University of Malta's MD (Doctor of Medicine and Surgery) directly after Class XII?
faq.answer: Not confirmed as a direct pathway. The University of Malta's own India-specific admissions page directs holders of the Indian School Certificate / Higher Secondary Certificate / All India Senior School Certificate Examination to its International School for Foundation Studies pathway programmes, rather than confirming direct entry into the 5-year MD course. The documented route into medicine for such applicants is the one-year Certificate in Foundation Studies in Medical and Dental Sciences, and admission to that foundation course is itself discretionary, decided via interview and/or a written test. Students should contact the University's admissions office directly to confirm the exact route for their specific qualification.

faq.question: Is the University of Malta MD programme taught entirely in English?
faq.answer: Course content is taught in English, but the university requires all students -- including international ones -- to also meet a Medical Maltese proficiency requirement before starting Year 1 and again before Year 3 of the course, unless they qualify for a specific exemption. This typically means passing a Secondary Education Certificate unit in Maltese, obtaining the University's Medical Maltese Proficiency Certificate, or completing the Medical Maltese unit within an approved foundation course. This is a genuine, separate requirement from general English-medium instruction and should be planned for in advance.

faq.question: How much does the University of Malta MD programme cost for international students?
faq.answer: The official course page lists an annual tuition fee of EUR 26,000 for the 5-year, 300-ECTS Doctor of Medicine and Surgery course, current at the time of research. This is notably higher than several other popular MBBS-abroad destinations, so it should be factored into total-cost planning. Students should reconfirm the exact current-year fee, and whether it is subject to annual review, directly with the University of Malta before applying.

faq.question: Will an MD from the University of Malta be recognised in India, and can graduates practise there?
faq.answer: This page does not assert automatic recognition. Practising in India after any foreign medical degree, including this one, requires meeting India's National Medical Commission (NMC) regulations for foreign medical graduates -- generally including qualifying NEET before enrolling abroad and clearing the Foreign Medical Graduate Examination (FMGE) or its successor exam (NExT) to obtain an Indian licence afterward. Because NMC's recognition rules and lists can change, students must verify current requirements directly with NMC before enrolling, rather than assuming blanket recognition based on general MBBS-abroad guidance.

faq.question: Where do University of Malta medical students live, and does the university run its own hostel?
faq.answer: The University of Malta does not operate its own halls of residence for medical students. It recommends that international students book Campus Hub Residence, a privately run student residence adjacent to the Msida campus, primarily because doing so simplifies the paperwork needed for a residence permit compared with arranging private rental housing independently. The University explicitly states it is not responsible for conditions at Campus Hub, since it is run by a third-party operator, so students should confirm current pricing, room types, and terms directly with Campus Hub or the University's accommodation office.

faq.question: Is this the same programme as the Queen Mary University of London (Barts) MBBS in Malta?
faq.answer: No. The University of Malta's Doctor of Medicine and Surgery (MD) programme, based in Msida, is a distinct, separate programme from the Queen Mary University of London (Barts and The London School of Medicine and Dentistry) MBBS/MBBCh programme, which is based on Gozo and awards a UK (QMUL) degree under its own admissions process. Applicants should be careful not to confuse the two when researching Malta as a medical-study destination, since they have different curricula, degrees awarded, fees, and admissions routes.

faq.question: What is Msida like as a place to live while studying medicine?
faq.answer: Msida is a small, central coastal town on Malta's northeast harbour area, about 2.5 kilometres from the capital, Valletta, and it hosts the University of Malta's main campus. It is generally described as quieter and more residential than the nearby Sliema and St Julian's districts, which supply most of the area's restaurants and nightlife within easy reach. Malta's Mediterranean climate, English-language accessibility, and generally lower cost of living relative to major Western European capitals make Msida a manageable base for international students, though students should still budget for EU-level living costs.
